sanitizedQuestion.questionText: Based on the passage, what is a potential long-term consequence of relying heavily on industrial monoculture farms?
sanitizedQuestion.passage:
The Growing Appeal of Urban Farming

As cities continue to expand, so does the innovative concept of urban farming – the practice of cultivating, processing, and distributing food in or around urban areas. This movement addresses several critical challenges faced by modern societies. By transforming rooftops, vacant lots, and even vertical spaces into productive farms, urban agriculture reduces the carbon footprint associated with long-distance food transportation. It also lessens reliance on industrial monoculture farms, which often deplete soil nutrients and contribute to biodiversity loss.

Beyond environmental benefits, urban farming fosters stronger community ties. Local residents often participate in planting, harvesting, and distributing produce, creating shared green spaces and educational opportunities. Access to fresh, healthy, and affordable food is significantly improved for urban dwellers, particularly in 'food deserts' where nutritious options are scarce. This localized food system promotes food security, supports local economies through job creation, and can even contribute to a city's aesthetic appeal. While challenges like limited space and initial investment exist, the multifaceted benefits of urban farming make it an increasingly vital component of sustainable urban development.

answerOptions.text: An increase in local biodiversity in agricultural areas.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states monoculture farms 'contribute to biodiversity loss,' so an increase in biodiversity is incorrect.

answerOptions.text: Improved soil health and nutrient content over time.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states monoculture farms 'often deplete soil nutrients,' so improved soil health is incorrect.

answerOptions.text: A reduction in the overall carbon footprint of food production.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage implies that urban farming reduces the carbon footprint by 'lessen[ing] reliance on industrial monoculture farms,' suggesting monoculture farms typically have a larger carbon footprint.

answerOptions.text: Degradation of soil quality and a reduction in species diversity in agricultural regions.
answerOptions.rationale: The passage states that industrial monoculture farms 'often deplete soil nutrients and contribute to biodiversity loss,' which directly points to degradation of soil quality and reduced species diversity as long-term consequences.

sanitizedQuestion.questionText: According to the passage, what is a fundamental implication of the theory of natural selection regarding species?
sanitizedQuestion.passage:
On the Origin of Species (Long Excerpt)

Charles Darwin, 1859

As many more individuals of each species are born than can possibly survive, and as there is consequently a frequent struggle for existence, it follows that any being, if it vary in any manner profitable to itself, will have a better chance of surviving, and thus be naturally selected. It is the preservation of favorable variations and the rejection of injurious variations. From the strong principle of inheritance, any selected variety will tend to propagate its new and modified form. The theory of natural selection, though simple in statement, carries profound consequences: it unites all forms of life in one long, unbroken chain of descent. No longer can we consider species as fixed creations; rather, they are the results of a gradual process, acting silently but surely over vast ages. The grandeur of this view of life lies in the unity of its origin and the endless forms most beautiful that have been, and are being, evolved.

On the Origin of Species (Long Excerpt)

Charles Darwin, 1859

As many more individuals of each species are born than can possibly survive, and as there is consequently a frequent struggle for existence, it follows that any being, if it vary in any manner profitable to itself, will have a better chance of surviving, and thus be naturally selected. sanitizedQuestion.questionText: According to the passage, what is the fundamental condition that allows for natural selection to occur?
sanitizedQuestion.passage:
On the Origin of Species (Long Excerpt)

Charles Darwin, 1859

As many more individuals of each species are born than can possibly survive, and as there is consequently a frequent struggle for existence, it follows that any being, if it vary in any manner profitable to itself, will have a better chance of surviving, and thus be naturally selected. It is the preservation of favorable variations and the rejection of injurious variations. From the strong principle of inheritance, any selected variety will tend to propagate its new and modified form. The theory of natural selection, though simple in statement, carries profound consequences: it unites all forms of life in one long, unbroken chain of descent. No longer can we consider species as fixed creations; rather, they are the results of a gradual process, acting silently but surely over vast ages. The grandeur of this view of life lies in the unity of its origin and the endless forms most beautiful that have been, and are being, evolved.
